parts.heading: From Hospital to University
parts.body:
Imagine training to be a naval officer in buildings designed by Sir Christopher Wren and once intended to house disabled sailors. That's precisely what happened at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, which occupied the magnificent Greenwich Hospital structures.

These grand buildings, constructed between 1696 and 1712, served as a home for pensioners until 1869. After the hospital closed and its residents were relocated, the impressive site became available for a new, equally significant purpose.

The Royal Naval College, Greenwich, was founded by an Order in Council on January 16, 1873. It was established as "the university of the Navy," replacing many of the functions previously performed by the Royal Naval Academy at Portsmouth.
parts.pullQuote: The Royal Naval College, Greenwich, was founded by an Order in Council on January 16, 1873.

parts.heading: Scholarly Endeavours
parts.body:
The new college's establishment included a president, always a flag officer, and a captain from the Royal Navy. On the academic side, it boasted a director of studies and professors covering a diverse range of subjects: mathematics, physical science, chemistry, applied mechanics, and fortification.

The director of studies, a civilian, oversaw an academic board, ensuring the quality of the courses offered. Officers entering the college were already sub-lieutenants, ready for advanced instruction. The captain of the college, a naval officer, served as the chief of staff.

Beyond its core offerings, the Royal Naval War College, which had been established at Greenwich in November 1900 before moving elsewhere, transferred its activities back to the Greenwich college in 1914. This move further solidified its role as a premier naval educational institution.

parts.heading: Wartime Transformations
parts.body:
The outbreak of World War I brought significant changes to the Royal Naval College. It was requisitioned to serve as a barracks and a hub for scientific experiments, temporarily halting the regular training of officers.

Officer training did not resume until 1919, after the war concluded. However, the college would soon play an even more critical role in another global conflict.

On October 30, 1939, as World War II began, the college expanded its mission to include the training of officers for the Women's Royal Naval Service. This period saw a dramatic increase in the number of both male and female officers trained for an expanded Navy, with approximately 35,000 men and women graduating, primarily as fighting officers.

parts.heading: Under Fire and Into the Nuclear Age
parts.body:
Even amidst the vital training effort during World War II, the college did not escape the war's destructive reach. In 1943, the beautiful Admiral's House, located on the north wing of King Charles Court, suffered a direct hit from a German bomb.

Another bomb also struck the front of the main building, a stark reminder of the conflict raging outside its walls. Despite these attacks, the college continued its crucial work.

Years later, in 1959, the Navy's Department of Nuclear Science and Technology opened on the college premises, marking a significant step into a new era of naval warfare. JASON, the department's research and training reactor, was commissioned in the King William building in 1962.

parts.heading: Royal Knighthood and Departures
parts.body:
The college's prominent riverside location also served as a backdrop for a moment of national celebration. In 1967, Queen Elizabeth II knighted Francis Chichester on the river steps of the college.

This honour recognized Chichester's extraordinary achievement as the first solo yachtsman to circumnavigate the world via the old clipper route. His nine-month and one-day journey was also the fastest such circumnavigation, a remarkable feat.

That same year, the Royal School of Naval Architecture, which had been an integral part of the college since 1873, transferred its operations to University College London. Later, in 1976, the training of women officers at the Royal Naval College also ceased, as their courses were moved to the Britannia Royal Naval College.

parts.heading: Closing Chapters
parts.body:
By 1983, the relocated Joint Services Defence College began to occupy a significant portion of the King Charles building, sharing the historic site.

However, the Royal Naval College's long and distinguished history at Greenwich was drawing to a close. With a shrinking Royal Navy, the difficult decision was made to close RNC Greenwich in 1998.

Today, all initial officer training for the Royal Navy takes place at the Britannia Royal Naval College. The staff college functions were assumed by the new Joint Services Command and Staff College, created in 1997, marking the end of an era for Greenwich.

parts.heading: A Palace's Legacy
parts.body:
The story of the Royal Naval College's buildings stretches back even further than its naval occupation. The site itself holds centuries of history, having once been home to the medieval Palace of Placentia, or the "Palace at Greenwich."

This palace was begun by Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester, in 1428, long before Wren's magnificent structures graced the riverfront. It was here, on this very ground, that King Henry VIII was born in 1491, adding another layer of royal significance to the location.

After the Royal Navy's departure in 1998, these historic buildings, now known as the Old Royal Naval College, were opened to the public, allowing everyone to explore their rich past.

parts.heading: Distinguished Alumni and Modern Uses
parts.body:
Over its long tenure, the Royal Naval College educated many notable figures who went on to shape naval history and beyond. Among its students were Admiral of the Fleet David Beatty, 1st Earl Beatty, and Admiral of the Fleet Lord Cunningham of Hyndhope.

Even members of royalty, such as Admiral of the Fleet Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, and Prince Charles, Count of Flanders, Prince-Regent of Belgium, walked its halls. These individuals, and many others, carried the college's legacy into service across the globe.

Since its decommissioning, the Old Royal Naval College has continued its public life, hosting attractions like the Painted Hall, the chapel, and the Discover Greenwich visitor centre. Its stunning architecture has also made it a popular film location, appearing in movies such as *Sherlock Holmes* (2009) and *Thor: The Dark World*.
parts.pullQuote: Among its students were Admiral of the Fleet David Beatty, 1st Earl Beatty, and Admiral of the Fleet Lord Cunningham of Hyndhope.

timeline.label: College founded by Order in Council
